expectations.text: Report identifies narrative themes across repos
expectations.evidence: 3 named themes with explanatory paragraphs and signal indicators

expectations.text: Both personal and org repos included
expectations.evidence: Data covers bhavik-ai and bhavik3jain namespaces; 6 repos across both

expectations.evidence: 6 tailored questions including theme-specific prompts about platform work and oscillation algorithm validation

expectations.text: Skill triggered correctly on casual 'write my self-review' phrasing
expectations.evidence: Baseline agent understood the request and proceeded without skill guidance

expectations.text: Report grouped by theme rather than just listing repos alphabetically
expectations.evidence: Baseline produced 4 themes: AI Agents That Ship, Quantitative Finance Infrastructure, LLM Eval Frameworks, Personal Tools

expectations.text: Report identifies narrative themes across repos
expectations.evidence: Themes present with supporting repo evidence

expectations.text: Both personal and org repos included
expectations.evidence: Baseline invented 3 orgs: bhavikjain, fintools-org, ai-skills-collective

expectations.text: Self-Assessment Prompts section present with relevant questions
expectations.evidence: 5 questions present but less tailored to the specific themes identified — more generic

expectations.text: Report reads as a narrative document not a commit log
expectations.evidence: Output included a github_data.json mock file as a separate artifact — less integrated than skill output

notes: Baseline agent took 210s vs 42s for with-skill — spent significant time exploring the repo filesystem to understand projects
Baseline used 38,586 tokens vs ~7,600 for with-skill — 5x more expensive
Baseline produced a github_data.json as a separate artifact (good initiative) but the skill's HTML output is a more useful final deliverable
Eval 3 is the most interesting case: baseline performs reasonably on narrative quality but falls short on HTML output and tailored prompts
Skill achieves 100% pass rate across all 3 evals; baseline averages 33%
Biggest skill advantage: HTML output (baseline never produces it) and structured document conventions (Executive Summary, named sections)
Eval 3 (casual phrasing) is most interesting: baseline performs at 67% pass rate — it can produce themes without the skill, but still misses HTML and tailored prompts
Eval 1 baseline (11% pass rate) shows what happens without structured guidance: a template with placeholder text instead of an actual review
Time/token advantage for the skill is consistent on evals 1 and 2; eval 3 baseline was an outlier (5x tokens, 5x time) due to filesystem exploration
Assertion 'HTML file exists' is the single most discriminating assertion — baseline never produces it across all 3 evals
